statement: LM-Design improves state-of-the-art results by 4% to 12% accuracy gains in sequence recovery on single-chain benchmarks
GoT achieves significant improvement over the strong CoT baseline on the AQUA-RAT test set
The model achieves improvements over BART baseline on CNN/DailyMail abstractive summarization
LaRS processes example banks four times faster than baseline methods
LaRS reduces LLM inferences during the selection stage by half
DKI significantly outperforms RAG by +10-16% at user context lengths >2500 tokens
Training Wikipedia2Vec took 6 hours on 32 CPUs, and fitting the linear transformation is negligible in cost
Performance monotonically improves with datastore size; a model trained on 100M tokens with 1.6B-token datastore already surpasses the performance of a model trained on all 3B tokens
Pre-training was conducted for 200k steps on 64 Google Cloud TPUs with batch size 512
Prompt tuning matches the strong performance of model tuning (where all model weights are tuned) as model size exceeds billions of parameters, closing the gap on SuperGLUE.
Prompt-tuned T5-Small matches GPT-3 XL (over 400× larger), and prompt-tuned T5-Large beats GPT-3 175B (over 100× larger) on SuperGLUE few-shot.
Synthesis performance scales log-linearly with model size across evaluated models from 244M to 137B parameters
CSD adds an average of 8% overhead to the sampling procedure
RETRO obtains comparable performance to GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 on the Pile, despite using 25× fewer parameters
Performance gain increases with retrieval database size and plateaus beyond 40 neighbours on C4 validation set
RETRO outperforms baseline transformer on majority of Pile test subsets; on dm_mathematics and ubuntu_irc, retrieved neighbours are not helpful and model underperforms baseline
performance steadily improves when increasing memory size up to 262K tokens
a Memorizing Transformer with 8k tokens in memory matches the perplexity of a vanilla Transformer with 5X more trainable parameters
step time increased from 0.2s to 0.25s with 8K memory and to 0.6s with 65K memory for a 200M parameter model
On 64-shot KILT tasks, Atlas with Perplexity Distillation achieves 45.0% exact match on NaturalQuestions, 15.0% F1 on Wizard of Wikipedia, and 77.0% accuracy on FEVER
Query-side fine-tuning (fixing document encoder parameters) avoids index refresh overhead with minimal performance impact in few-shot settings
Index refresh overhead during pre-training is around 30% compared to training with a fixed retriever, when refreshing every 1,000 steps out of 10,000 total pre-training iterations
tuned lens predictions have substantially lower perplexity than logit lens predictions across language models with up to 20B parameters
strong correlation (Spearman ρ) between causal influence of features on tuned lens and their influence on the model itself
tuned lens prediction depth correlates with iteration learned (difficulty metric) on all 11 tasks investigated; outperforms logit lens on 8 out of 11 tasks
With GPT-3.5, Logic-LM outperforms standard LLM on all datasets with an average improvement of 39.2%
GPT-4 achieves executable rate (Exe_Rate) of 79.9% on FOLIO and 32.6% on AR-LSAT
ITI locates truthful directions using only few hundred examples

sense vector ablation: Mini-sized Backpack models with sense counts k=1,2,4,8 achieve OpenWebText perplexities of 38.6, 29.3, 26.0, and 24.1 respectively, demonstrating the necessity of a non-singleton set of sense vectors
LEACE achieves random chance accuracy (perfect erasure) on gender prediction from BERT embeddings with minimal edit distance, while RLACE comes close and INLP does not
LEACE is approximately 2 orders of magnitude faster than RLACE and does not require gradient-based optimization

Erasing part-of-speech information via LEACE concept scrubbing induces perplexity increases of 1.73–3.57 bits per UTF-8 byte across LLaMA (7B–30B) and Pythia (160M–12B) models, compared to SAL which induces 3.16–4.69 bits
ActAdd requires minimal data: steering vectors are computed from a single pair of contrast prompts (e.g., 'Love' vs 'Hate')
LAT outperforms few-shot prompting on traditional QA benchmarks (OpenbookQA, CommonSenseQA, RACE, ARC) by a notable margin.
LAT outperforms prior methods such as CCS (Burns et al., 2022) by a wide margin on DeBERTa evaluated on common benchmarks.
Sparse dictionary features are far more interpretable by autointerpretability score than dictionary features found by comparable techniques (default basis, random directions, PCA, ICA)

Patching sparse dictionary features requires fewer patches to reach a given level of KL divergence from target compared to PCA decomposition and non-sparse dictionary on the Indirect Object Identification task
Strong causal effects of FVs are observed across models ranging from 6B to 70B parameters, with effects concentrated in middle layers

CAA significantly alters model behavior on multiple-choice behavioral evaluation datasets across all seven tested behaviors (AI Coordination, Corrigibility, Hallucination, Myopic Reward, Survival Instinct, Sycophancy, Refusal)
CAA can modify model behavior beyond what is achieved through system-prompting alone for most behaviors tested
For 3 out of 7 tested behaviors, CAA can additionally steer the behavior beyond the effects of supervised finetuning alone in both positive and negative directions
Generating a CAA steering vector requires less than five minutes on a single GPU, whereas finetuning Llama 2 7B Chat on 1000 examples requires 10 minutes on 2 NVIDIA L40 GPUs
For ROME, the efficacy score is almost 100% until a point where it begins to decline, which can come as early as 100 edits or as late as 1000 edits.

MEMIT maintains ability to perform downstream tasks on GPT-J as more edits are made, but shows gradual loss of downstream performance on GPT2-XL with multiple edits, with the model losing ability on paraphrase detection long before catastrophic forgetting point.
Graph encoder parameters range from 17,152 to 198,788 parameters depending on architecture choice; projection layer adds approximately 11 million parameters total

SelfIE achieves the same performance on eliciting LLM's internal representation of world state in TextWorld as prior supervised approach trained on 100-shot samples, demonstrating the effectiveness and faithfulness of our zero-shot readout approach.

ASAp produces correct samples 38% more often than GCD (geomean) on INV-BV benchmarks; however on SLIA benchmarks that both tools can solve, ASAp produces correct samples 73% less often than GCD (geomean)
TopK autoencoders empirically outperform baseline ReLU autoencoders on the sparsity-reconstruction frontier, with this gap increasing with scale.
The number of tokens to convergence increases approximately as D^1.4 for GPT-2 small and D^1.5 for GPT-4, where D is the number of latents.
Joint scaling law fit on GPT-4 autoencoders: MSE follows power law with coefficients α_d ≈ -0.65, α_k ≈ -0.28, β_d ≈ -0.23, β_k ≈ -0.05, γ_d ≈ 2.0, γ_k ≈ 0.5, including irreducible loss term.
Removing schema restriction from format-restricting prompts significantly improves average scores on GSM8K across Claude-3-Haiku, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct with lower standard deviations
NL-to-Format shows nearly identical performance to unrestricted Natural Language responses across most models, with only minor degradation in some cases
The bridge entity is successfully decoded from the first-hop position across models in 41%-78% of correctly answered cases using Patchscopes.
The target entity is decoded from the final token position in 71%-86% of cases where the model predicted correctly.
Critical information propagates from the first-hop position to the final token position in the majority of cases (detected via attention knockout, projection, or Patchscopes).
SAE latents underperform linear regression probes on first-letter identification across Gemma Scope SAEs, with no SAE matching LR probe performance.
Feature absorption rate increases with higher sparsity (L0) and higher SAE width; lower L0 pushes the SAE to absorb dense features like spelling information.
KBLaM enables integrating a large KB of more than 10K triples into an 8B pre-trained LLM of only 8K context window on one single A100 80GB GPU
KBLaM shows performance comparable to in-context learning on synthetic data while using much smaller memory footprint
On in-distribution synthetic data, KBLaM's attention achieves high top-1 and top-5 accuracy in identifying relevant triples
On out-of-distribution Enron data, KBLaM shows degraded performance compared to synthetic data but still performs better than zero-shot baseline
KBLaM degrades slower in precision for detecting unanswerable questions as KB size increases compared to in-context learning
Fuzzing and detection are at least 5x cheaper than simulation scoring when using the all-at-once (AAO) method
Token-by-token (TBT) simulation scoring is 30x more expensive than both fuzzing and detection
Interpreting 1 million features using the described setup costs $200
CB-LLM w/ ACC achieves test accuracy competitive with or matching finetuned black-box RoBERTa-base across SST2, YelpP, AGnews, and DBpedia
CB-LLM w/ ACC achieves higher average human rating on Activation Faithfulness task compared to TBMC3M baseline
CB-LLM (generation) achieves concept detection accuracy within ~3% of Llama3-8B finetuned for direct concept classification
CB-LLM (generation) achieves significantly higher steerability score with adversarial training (Module 2) versus without, where steerability without adversarial training is close to random
CB-LLM (generation) achieves perplexity comparable to standard finetuned Llama3-8B, indicating no degradation in generation quality
Models trained using 1.6B parameter models and training data in the order of 1.3T tokens
One architecture scaled to a model size of 7B parameters and training data of about 2.7T tokens
SaT Capped sentence segmentation method with 200 character capping achieves higher AutoBLEU scores than SpaCy Capped across all sentence lengths
Increasing the number of SAE latents decreases the overlap between different seeds

Significantly increases functional correctness in code synthesis, translation, and repair tasks across LLMs of various sizes and model families, including state-of-the-art open-weight models with more than 30B parameters
Trained for 800 billion tokens at 3.5 billion parameters on Frontier supercomputer with 4096 GPUs
ASP has execution-rate ranging from 62% to 95% and maintains overall-accuracy above 30%, while Pyke has most ablation scenarios below 70% overall-accuracy.
Conceptualized models maintain classification performance on par with or slightly better than the original model across AG News, Yelp, and DBpedia datasets.
a 2-layer transformer trained from scratch with Coconut achieves near-perfect accuracy on ProsQA, while both CoT and No CoT baselines solve only about 75% of the tasks
a latent vector of just 32 dimensions is sufficient to achieve high-fidelity reconstruction, with a token-level accuracy of over 99.9% when grouping K=4 tokens
BrierLM exhibits a Pearson correlation coefficient of -0.966 and a Spearman's rank correlation of -0.991 with cross-entropy loss

In GPT-4o evaluations on 512 generated stories, SONAR-LLM 900M outperforms both diffusion-based and MSE-based LCM variants, achieving grammatical correctness of 7.1, creativity of 5.2, coherence of 6.0, and plot consistency of 5.2, compared to diffusion LCM (grammar 3.9, creativity 5.0, coherence 2.

SONAR-LLM 900M matches LLM performance on ROUGE-L with short prefixes (39.4 vs. 39.4) and achieves competitive METEOR (32.9 vs. 34.2). With longer context, SONAR-LLM attains comparable ROUGE-L (35.2 vs. 35.7), substantially outperforming MSE LCM (26.1 ROUGE-L) and diffusion LCM (31.2 ROUGE-L) on sho

On XSum, SONAR-LLM 1.3B achieves ROUGE-L of 18.5(0.7) and METEOR of 14.5(0.5). On CNN/DailyMail, SONAR-LLM 1.3B achieves ROUGE-L of 15.5(0.7) and METEOR of 10.0(0.4), substantially outperforming diffusion LCM (XSum: 12.0 ROUGE-L; CNN/DM: 10.1 ROUGE-L) and MSE LCM (XSum: 11.6 ROUGE-L; CNN/DM: 7.8 ROU
BALOR Show Rate (SR) in zero-shot color task: Llama2-7b 49.39%, Llama3.2-1b 71.66%, Llama3-8b 56.42%, Qwen3-4b 73.45% (BALOR Shared mode)
BALOR Show Rate (SR) in few-shot color task: Llama2-7b 52.10%, Llama3.2-1b 69.77%, Llama3-8b 47.62%, Qwen3-4b 68.90% (BALOR Shared mode)
Probe training requires under 5 hours on a single GPU with 8.4k–10k training questions
Middle layers (17–21) of DeepSeek-7B-Chat contain the most useful correctness signals for steering, with layers 17–21 yielding the strongest improvements in both accuracy and calibration
Format penalty scales with schema complexity (McNemar p < 0.0001)
KV-cache reuse consistently reduces latency by 3–4× and GPU memory usage by 2–3× across all datasets

Process supervision significantly outperforms outcome supervision for training models to solve problems from the challenging MATH dataset. The PRM reaches higher performance for all values of N, and the performance gap widens as N increases.
Process supervision significantly outperforms both forms of outcome supervision at all data collection scales (1 to 200 solutions per problem).
The PRM outperforms both the ORM and majority voting on out-of-distribution STEM tests (AP Physics, AP Calculus, AP Chemistry, AMC10/12) with 100 test samples per problem.
CFPRM improves upon ReSTMCTS* by 1.3% on GSM-Plus and 3.4% on MATH500 when employing MSE as the loss function
CFPRM consistently brings performance improvements across all configurations (BoN@8, BoN@16, BoN@32, BoN@64) across three loss criteria (BCE, MSE, Q-ranking) on two datasets
When integrating CFPRM with the Q-ranking-based method (PQM), performance on GSM-Plus averages to 71.3% (BoN) and on MATH500 averages to 84.1% (BoN)

PRM-guided search based on DeepSeekMath achieves over 20% higher performance than repeated sampling when sample size is 1, but by sample size of 16, verifier-guided search becomes inferior to repeated sampling, reaching approximately 5% lower performance when scaled to 32
OVM-guided search performance degradation on GSM8K is approximately 10%, increasing to around 20% on MATH (measured at sample size 32 vs. sample size 1)
Scaling flaws are more severe on OOD problems: OVM-guided search performance degradation increases from 19.3% on in-distribution Level 4 to 25.8% in OOD-L4 setting at sample size 32
A large proportion of OVM-guided search failures occur during the selection stage: 88.6% on GSM8K (Mistral), 80.0% on MATH (DeepSeekMath), 84.3% on OOD-L4, and 81.2% on OOD-L5

One-time Monte Carlo rollout with λ=1 (full reliance on simulated reward) yields the highest accuracy gain, underscoring that relying entirely on rollout reward without verifier-predicted score outperforms verifier evaluation

VisualPRM improves the overall reasoning performance of MiniCPM-V2.6, QwenVL2.5-7B, InternVL2.5-8B, and InternVL2.5-78B by 8.0, 3.7, 8.4, and 5.9 points, respectively, across seven multimodal reasoning benchmarks
Among 7B/8B-scale reasoning LLMs, Tango-Qwen-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance averaging 49.5% accuracy on math tasks and 62.8% on out-of-domain tasks
BiPRM introduces only 0.3% parameter increase for the gating module compared to L2R-PRM baseline
Parallel execution of bidirectional streams incurs approximately 5% wall-clock inference time latency increase compared to unidirectional L2R baseline
VRPRM-MiMo achieves average AEI of 66.44 and average FEI of 51.54, outperforming VisualPRM by 5.41 in AEI and 25.93 in FEI

On a synthetic binary-tree environment with imbalanced process rewards, λ-GRPO converges on the target high-reward path more frequently than standard GRPO across all hyperparameter configurations tested (ε ∈ {-0.5, -1.0, -1.5}, K ∈ {1, 2}).

λ-GRPO-tuned models outperformed standard GRPO on 15 out of 20 downstream reasoning task cells (AIME24, MATH-500, AMC23, Minerva, OlympiadBench) across two model sizes (Qwen-1.5B and Llama-1B) and two KL coefficient settings.
On equivalence-based answers from SCI-VerifyBench, SCI-Verifier-8B achieves substantially higher performance than GPT-5, particularly in mathematics and physics where GPT-5 scores drop below 50%
On WebQSP, DPRM achieves F1 score of 0.7228 and Hit@1 of 0.8715
On CWQ, DPRM achieves F1 score of 0.5988 and Hit@1 of 0.7301
Removing co-training drops F1 on CWQ by 0.0569 and Hit@1 by 0.0477
RPM-MCTS achieves an average improvement of 11.90% on Qwen3-8B, 7.71% on Qwen3-235B-A22B, and 10.86% on Claude Sonnet 3.7 across all benchmarks
Ablation study (w/o ER, removing execution rewards) resulted in the largest overall performance drop

FunPRM outperforms baselines across all four base LLMs (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-Coder-33B, Qwen2.5-7B-Coder) and achieves best overall performance in 18 out of 20 settings on LiveCodeBench and BigCodeBench subsets
On LiveCodeBench (2025-02-01 present) with O4-mini (High), FunPRM achieves 100% on Easy (31 problems), 92.3% on Medium (39 problems), and 63.9% on Hard (61 problems)

Ablation study on LiveCodeBench (2025-02-01 present) shows FunPRM full model achieves 80.9% overall, FunPRM without Chain-of-Function achieves 80.1%, and FunPRM without meta reward correction achieves 80.2%
Ground-truth test inputs consistently outperform randomly generated inputs by a large margin on USACO, with random generation failing to close the performance gap even at scale

Agentic Verifier produces 8349 tokens over 11.3 rounds with 1.6 tool calls per verification trajectory, requiring 323.4 seconds latency on A100 GPU (batch size 128), compared to 2560 tokens and 119.0 seconds for the base model
VHG raises Pass@1 on indefinite integral from 28.8% (Qwen3-4B-Base) to 45.4% (VHG with hard verifier) on Competition, from 52.5% to 69.4% on Qualifier, and from 43.3% to 64.7% on Integral Stress Test
On general math benchmarks, VHG raises overall pass@1 accuracy from 56.8% to 69.0% across MATH, AMC, Minerva, Olympiad, and AIME24-26
On indefinite integral, VHG-generated problems verifier-accepted and zero pass-rate remain challenging for larger models: Qwen3-32B achieves Pass@1 of 46.99% and Pass@8 of 85.99%
On general math, VHG-generated zero pass-rate problems show Pass@1 of 41.31% and Pass@8 of 70.71% for Qwen3-32B (8× larger than setter/solver)
Symbolic bounding box meta-verification reduces per-card GPU memory usage from 56.9 GB to 48.6 GB compared to textual explanations during training on OmniVerifier-7B.
M1-TTS with OmniVerifier-M1 and GPT-Image-1.5 achieves 0.88 on WISE overall and 0.800 on T2I-CoreBench overall, outperforming Qwen3-VL-8B+GPT-Image-1.5 baseline at 0.86 and 0.787 respectively.
Domain adaptation via automatically extracted terminology with GBS yields approximately 1.8 BLEU points improvement for EN-DE and 2.6 BLEU points for EN-FR on Autodesk Post-Editing Corpus
DBA achieves O(1) complexity in the number of constraints, whereas prior work (Grid Beam Search and Constrained Beam Search) achieves linear or exponential complexity
PaD accomplishes comparable performance utilizing merely 4.5% of the baseline model's data size
The average percentage of inconsistent APIs in zero-shot prompting baselines ranges from 60% to 90%
FLAP on Mistral-7b-instruct outperforms larger LLMs (30B-40B parameter range) in flow step and API error reduction
Control-DAG on DA-T5-small achieves BLEURT 60.0 and BLEU 22.9 on SGD
Control-DAG on DA-T5-small achieves BLEURT 46.8 and BLEU 19.0 on DART with zero exact occurrence error rate (EOR=0.0) and zero neologism rate (NEO=0.0)
Every model with constrained decoding shows better secure-pass@ than GPT-4 with Nucleus Sampling, tested over six open-source Code LLMs of sizes ranging from 2.7B to 34B.
The proposed method largely outperforms previous studies using lexically constrained decoding on oracle constraints.
The proposed method achieved significantly higher constraint satisfaction rates compared to Zetsu et al. (2022) on oracle constraints.

Manual error analysis on ASSET shows the proposed method decreased errors in failed named entity generation, dropped information in complex sentences, and altered meaning/focus compared to Zetsu et al. (2022).
With predicted constraints, the proposed method consistently outperformed baselines on SARI across all three corpora, while previous methods (Agrawal et al. 2021, Kajiwara 2019) showed instability.
Constraint prediction precision on substitution operations was low, with room for improvement particularly in deletion and substitution operations.
CDSL achieves 2.2x to 12.15x speedup over CDLH without significant performance reduction
On CommonGen, CDSL achieves hard constraint satisfaction gain compared to greedy decoding
On Harmless Text Generation, CDSL achieves gain compared to greedy decoding
CDLH-Appx. baseline outperforms greedy by in CommonGen and in HTG tasks

RCD achieves STL satisfaction improvements of 8-24 percentage points over unconstrained baselines while preserving success rates much closer to unconstrained level with drops of 0.5–6.5 percentage points
Inference overhead of STL evaluation via STLCG++ is minimal at approximately 0.005 s per timestep
STATIC achieves 47-1033x speedup over hardware-accelerated binary-search baseline (PPV)

In online A/B test on YouTube Home Feed with 7-day freshness constraint: 7-day fresh video views increased by [value not stated in extracted text], 3-day fresh views increased by [value not stated], click-through rate increased by [value not stated], and strategic user segment satisfaction improved

BIRD-dev set contains 61 templates covering 134 queries out of 1534 total queries; Spider-dev set contains 482 templates covering 966 queries out of 1034 total queries in real (non-synthesized) benchmark data
compression ratios ranging from 40:1 (Llama-3B/C++) to 800:1 (GPT-20B/JSON)
XGrammar2 increases inference time over the base model by factors of 2x and 10x for C++ and Bython, respectively
CFGzip cuts the overhead of XGrammar2 by one to two orders of magnitude; nearly halves the total inference time on C++, and reduces total inference time on Bython by a factor of 7.5x
On xLAM function calling with JSON format, P-GCD achieves substantially higher accuracy than LCD at the same number of SMC particles, converging faster to the rejection-sampling ground truth.
On CommonGen keyword-based generation, P-GCD as a potential function achieves substantially higher BLEU-4 score than LCD proposals across a range of particle budgets.
On XSum with beam search (4), TP-Transformer (TPT-d) achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 35.84/14.06/28.40/25.79, outperforming standard Transformer at 34.46/12.97/27.21/24.42
On Wikihow with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 36.70/13.75/27.36/22.53, outperforming Transformer at 35.91/13.49/27.01/21.57
On Arxiv with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves ROUGE-1/ROUGE-2/ROUGE-L/METEOR of 38.36/11.43/34.29/23.61, outperforming Transformer at 38.01/11.33/34.02/23.19
On CNN/Daily Mail with beam search (4), TP-Transformer achieves 41.01/18.38/37.91/34.34, achieving competitive performance with Transformer at 41.22/18.70/38.09/34.50
On XSum human evaluation (120 samples), TP-Transformer wins 52 out of 120 on overall preference versus Transformer's 48, with TP-Transformer winning 47 samples on grammar versus Transformer's 39
The attention-based resonator network outperforms the traditional resonator network for multiple factors (k ≥ 3), with an increasing gap in performance as k increases
The attention-based update rule requires significantly fewer iterations to converge compared to the traditional resonator network
For FHRR hypervectors, the adapted original resonator network significantly underperforms compared to the attention-based resonator network

The attention-based FHRR and attention-based bipolar resonator networks achieve an order of magnitude lower complexity (average steps for correct convergence) compared to the original bipolar resonator network
Projecting LLM-derived semantic vectors (768 real numbers) to 1k bit VSA vectors results in compression.

For semantic matching (Russia-Ukraine, Stocks, Warthunder topics), multiple-vector 1k-bit representation achieved accuracy of 1.0, 0.993, and 0.997 respectively; single-vector 10k-bit achieved 1.0, 0.973, and 0.983.
Sentiment classification accuracy was 95% for multiple-vector 1k-bit but dropped to 80% for single-vector 1k-bit; 10k-bit achieved 97% for both representations.

Timestamp ordering (level hypervectors method) achieved 99% accuracy for multiple-vector but degraded to close to random for single-vector 1k-bit; using basis vectors method, single-vector achieved 90% accuracy.

Pilot behavioral experiment (four LLMs, 2B–7B, 720 trials): HIS re-injection improves safety adherence at 2B scale (effect size d=0.60, p<0.05, 95% CI [0.22, 0.98]), with refusal rate improvement matching a matched-frequency timer baseline
3-layer Transformers with CoT achieve near-perfect accuracy on all four tasks (Arithmetic, Equation, LIS, and Edit Distance) for all difficulty levels
On GSM8K with self-consistency (maj@8), LaTRO achieves 90.5% accuracy for Phi-3.5, 73.8% for Mistral-7B, and 87.0% for Llama-3.1-8B
MC-dropout consistently achieves higher coverage across nearly all sample sizes, with coverage increasing monotonically as sample count increases, showing saturation effects at larger N.
At N=64 samples, COCONUT and CODI achieve nearly equivalent coverage despite CODI's clear superiority at N=1, indicating that increased sampling narrows inter-model performance gaps.
Best-of-N selection and Beam Search guided by LatentRM consistently outperform Majority Voting across compute budgets, with gains most pronounced on MultiArith.

Ablation on LatentRM shows step-wise contrastive loss contributes 1.9% improvement over BCE on GSM-Test and 0.4% on GSM-Hard; removing stochastic rollouts causes further 2.8% and 1.4% drops respectively; random untrained head performs below Majority Voting.
Deep Improvement Supervision reduces the total number of forward passes by 18× while maintaining performance on complex reasoning tasks.
LiteReason reduces final reasoning length by 77–92% while approaching non-latent RL training performance
During RL training, LiteReason requires 52.8% fewer discrete tokens on Flawed Fictions (29.0M vs. 61.4M) and 49.5% fewer on Next Chapter Prediction (54.9M vs. 108.8M) compared to standard RL-Trained
Models require 2 to 8x fewer training tokens for the same accuracy as FLOP-, parameter-, and memory-matched SOTA
DR+DA with depth 16 outperforms LA with depth 32 on all reasoning benchmarks
Depth-recurrent models use 2 to 11x larger expert selection diversity than SOTA MoEs
At depth 16: DR achieves 4.0x data efficiency; DR+DA achieves 2.9x data efficiency (average across benchmarks)
At depth 32: DR achieves 2.6x data efficiency; DR+DA achieves 3.5x data efficiency (average across benchmarks)

On molecular captioning with LLaMA-1B, ReGuLaR achieves state-of-the-art performance across BLEU-2, BLEU-4, METEOR, and ROUGE metrics with a single latent reasoning step, outperforming explicit CoT which uses hundreds of reasoning steps.
On COCONUT, mild dropout (p=0.1) achieves positive sampling gain (SG) with minimal JS divergence, whereas stronger dropout (p=0.5) leads to negative SG despite increased divergence.
GTS achieves stronger scaling behavior than dropout-based sampling and standard Gaussian noise baselines as sampling budget increases on both COCONUT and CODI.
Dense reward (correctness + confidence shaping) consistently outperforms accuracy-only reward for GTS training, with the performance gap widening as sampling budget increases.
SeLaR achieves the highest average accuracy, improving upon CoT (Sampling) by +0.75%, +3.88%, and +2.45% on Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B, respectively.
On Qwen3-8B, SeLaR improves AIME 2024 from 76.67% to 83.33% (+6.66%) and AIME 2025 from 66.67% to 80.00% (+13.33%).

Abstract-CoT generalizes across model families: on MATH-500, Qwen3-4B achieves 89.8% accuracy with 141 tokens and Granite 4.0 Micro (3B) achieves 74.4% accuracy with 153 tokens, both substantially reducing tokens compared to their verbal CoT baselines
LaSR does not introduce additional latency compared to standard SFT because it does not require token-by-token prefilling during decoding

GLR adds approximately 1M trainable parameters to Qwen3-0.6B and 4M trainable parameters to Qwen3-1.7B (the lightweight transition head), while freezing all model parameters and the input token-embedding layer.
<swi> achieves a probability of 0.203 and rank 1 at annotated switch positions vs. 0.068 probability and rank 1003.9 at random non-boundary positions (After SFT checkpoint)
Training a Gated SAE requires 50% more compute than training a matched-width baseline SAE due to dual decoder passes in the auxiliary loss.
Mistral 7B achieves 31/49 accuracy on weekdays modular arithmetic task and 125/144 on months task.
Llama 3 8B achieves 29/49 accuracy on weekdays task and 143/144 on months task.
Circular subspace intervention on Mistral 7B achieves nearly the same causal effect as patching the entire layer, with average logit difference approaching layer-patching baselines.
Transcoders achieve equal or better sparsity-accuracy Pareto frontier than SAEs across GPT2-small, Pythia-410M, and Pythia-1.4B, with the gap widening on larger models.
FPO achieves approximately 17.6% GPU memory reduction compared to TDPO-2 during training

Weight orthogonalization of the top unknown entity direction on every output matrix results in large reduction in refusal responses, demonstrating the direction plays a crucial role in knowledge refusal
RouteSAE achieves the best performance among all methods, maintaining a lower KL divergence at comparable sparsity levels
The H-SAE with 64 sublatents per expert achieves reconstruction performance on par with the standard SAE with 32 top-level features despite having half the compute cost
H-SAE shows lower feature absorption on first-letter classification benchmark compared to standard SAE
H-SAE uses more similar sets of features across languages than baseline SAE, measured by symmetric set difference of top 8 activated features on same tokens in different languages
Our method achieves the best skew values across all models, reaching an overall mismatch rate of just [0.06] on SD 1.4 while maintaining strong performance on SD 1.5 and SD 2.1.
SAFER group of top-ranked contrastive features exhibits significantly higher mean and median safety relevance scores (concentrated near maximum score of 5) compared to randomly sampled features.

SAFER achieves safety degradation of 83.11% (1B) and 86.89% (3B) at 0.5% poisoning rate while maintaining chat performance at 89.11% (1B) and 90.78% (3B), substantially outperforming Random baseline (93.24% / 91.90%) and matching Reward-based baseline on safety while preserving chat far better.
SAE-FNO achieves higher expressivity per concept and requires fewer effective concepts to represent the data population compared to SAE-MLP.
SAE-RSV yields substantially higher success rates across all tasks compared to LoRA-SFT (supervised fine-tuning)
With only 10 training samples, SAE-RSV matches the performance of CAA trained on 1000 samples
SparseRM achieves the highest accuracy on TruthfulQA and outperforms most baselines on SafeRLHF and Red-Teaming while using less than 1% of trainable parameters

Average cosine similarity between generated and training data is significantly lower in dense space (DenseRM) compared to sparse space (SparseRM), indicating more substantial distributional shift in dense representations
Margin loss consistently outperforms binary cross-entropy (BCE) loss and Bradley-Terry (BT) loss across all three datasets in reward modeling
The ConvNeXt-Tiny classifier achieves an accuracy of [value not stated] and a macro F1 score of [value not stated] on the test set
EKI-BART achieves 24.36 BLEU-2 and 45.42 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with in-domain prototype corpus, compared to BART baseline of 22.87 BLEU-2 and 41.44 BLEU-4
EKI-BART achieves 25.43 BLEU-2 and 46.53 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with out-of-domain prototype corpus (Wikipedia), compared to BART baseline of 23.15 BLEU-2 and 44.71 BLEU-4
All evaluation metrics show improvement: ROUGE-2/L, BLEU-3/4, METEOR, CIDEr, and SPICE all increase with EKI-BART across in-domain and out-of-domain settings

The no-magnitude predictor set improved cross-validated Spearman correlation over the frequency baseline by 0.14 in GPT-2-small, 0.16 in Pythia-70M, 0.08 in Gemma-2-2B, and 0.18 in Llama-3.1-8B, and over the activation-magnitude baseline by 0.10, 0.11, 0.07, and 0.13 respectively.

On downstream-count collateral, decoder crowding (top-k mean absolute cosine) reached univariate Spearman correlation of 0.35 in GPT-2-small, direct-logit footprint reached 0.32 in Pythia-70M, encoder norm reached 0.21 in Gemma-2-2B, and direct-logit footprint reached 0.34 in Llama-3.1-8B.

In GPT-2-small held-out screening on geometry-only selector (32 features per group), predicted-clean features were more stable than predicted-messy features (p=0.012), lower in collateral per effect (p=0.038), and did not differ significantly in effect magnitude (p=0.26).

In Pythia-70M held-out screening, the full no-magnitude selector produced a significant stability contrast (p=0.005, Δμ=0.061) on fresh contexts, while the collateral-per-effect contrast was not significant (p=0.50).

In Llama-3.1-8B held-out screening on the full no-magnitude selector (25 features per group), predicted-clean features had significantly lower collateral per effect (0.064 vs. 0.081, p=0.045) with no significant effect-magnitude difference (p=0.16), while the stability contrast was not significant (
General map-tuning training on NVIDIA Tesla V100 costs only 2 hours, compared to 3000+ GPU hours for knowledge-enhanced pre-training methods like LUKE and KEPLER
Map-tuning optimizes only 0.1% of parameters of the downstream model
Baichuan2-7B-Chat general model shows EX improvement of 2.8% and EM improvement of 2.5% with knowledge injection
Weight averaging initialization for expanded blocks produces lower performance drop (37.09 on TruthfulQA) compared to identity copy method (38.76) on MMLU and related benchmarks
Deleting up to 2 of the deepest layers is feasible without severely degrading model performance; deleting 4 layers degrades performance substantially
Prompt distillation is substantially more data-efficient than supervised fine-tuning; to match PD performance with 20 training questions, SFT requires an order of magnitude more training data
Prompt distillation's higher data efficiency compensates for its additional forward pass, making it more time-efficient than supervised fine-tuning
On NameDescription dataset, arLLM without paraphrases achieves 0.264 forward accuracy and 0.029 backward accuracy; with paraphrases, forward reaches 0.954 but backward remains 0.031.
dLLM (LLaDA-8B-Instruct) without paraphrases achieves 0.869 forward and 0.852 backward accuracy on NameDescription; with paraphrases, 0.980 forward and 0.984 backward.
Masked fine-tuning for arLLM on NameDescription achieves high accuracy in both forward and backward directions without paraphrases, closing the gap to dLLM performance.
On GSM8K, Llama-3.2-3B masked SFT achieves 0.735 pass@1, improving over baseline 0.686. On MATH, Llama-3.2-3B masked SFT achieves 0.290 pass@1 versus baseline 0.258.
Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements in knowledge adaptation (K.A) on EVOKE, outperforming LoRA by more than twofold
Kore (rank=235) achieves improvements of in Avg score and in HARS over the best baseline
On LLaVA-v1.5 (13B), Kore surpasses LoRA across all dimensions, achieving improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
On Qwen2.5-VL (7B), Kore achieves improvements of in Avg and in HARS over Replay
Ablation study: W/o Augmentation decreases knowledge adaptation (K.A) by a substantial margin
Kore-augmentation outperforms general augmentation methods for text (knowledge-aware) by in K.A, in Avg and in HARS
When trained solely for correctness, human contractor accuracy at evaluating solution validity (45-second time limit) decreases from initialization to 7k RL steps.
The helpful prover's pass rate improves over checkability training rounds and human judges evaluate helpful solutions correctly more often at higher rounds.
Verifiers show increasing robustness across five rounds of checkability training, measured by steps required until sneaky-only adversaries find exploits.
Human contractor accuracy when evaluating sneaky prover solutions decreases over checkability training rounds, indicating flaws become more subtle.
For CoTs labeled as correct by the auto-labeler, agreement rate with manual evaluations was 80% (95% CI: 0.69–0.89); for CoTs labeled as incorrect, agreement rate was 71% (95% CI: 0.63–0.79)
VersaPRM (random subset) achieves higher WMV accuracy across both math and non-math tasks compared to VersaPRM (Math subset), demonstrating the value of training on diverse data
MiPS significantly improves the performance of PaLM 2 on math and coding tasks: accuracy +0.67% on GSM8K, +4.16% on MATH, +0.92% on MBPP compared with an output supervision trained verifier
GenPRM significantly outperforms prior PRMs with only 23K training data from MATH dataset
A 1.5B GenPRM outperforms GPT-4o through test-time scaling
A 7B GenPRM surpasses Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B on ProcessBench
ThinkPRM-14B, trained on 8K process labels or 1K synthetic examples, outperforms discriminative PRMs trained on about 100x more data
ThinkPRM-1.5B, trained using the same 8K labels, outperforms LLM-as-a-judge and discriminative verifiers in reward-guided search on MATH-500
On ProcessBench, ThinkPRM substantially reduces invalid judgments (verification CoTs without extractable label) compared to LLM-as-a-Judge
LoVer enables a 7B-parameter LLM to achieve reasoning accuracy comparable to that of a 32B-parameter LLM
text-davinci-003 (175B) with DaSLaM decomposition produces competitive or better performance compared to GPT-4
improved self-correction abilities of two models on five datasets spanning math and commonsense reasoning
notable performance gains when paired with a strong GPT-4-based verifier
QCFG-constrained decoding with 3% training data (268 examples) achieves BLEU scores on par with unconstrained decoding using 100% of training data

On SMCalFlow validation data, QCFG random sampling achieves 100% truthfulness but only 56% relevance; unconstrained decoding achieves 97% relevance but 82% truthfulness; QCFG-constrained decoding achieves 97% relevance and 100% truthfulness

Grammar prompting achieves 98.0% validity, 0.74 diversity, 91.0% retrosynthesis score, and 93.3% membership for Acrylates molecule generation, compared to 87.7% validity, 0.73 diversity, 80.0% retrosynthesis score, and 76.7% membership for standard prompting
GreatGramma achieves an average 17.71x speedup in offline preprocessing compared to related approaches
GreatGramma maintains state-of-the-art online masking efficiency of 5-32ms per token
JamDec with GPT2-XL achieves the highest Task Score for almost every dataset, with Task Score on AMT-10 more than higher than almost all other methods on ENS and BertAA Task Score

Using CoDi-BS leads to an overall increase in Drop Rate of and an increase in the number of sentences that pass the base NLI and CoLA threshold of about, with little change in NLI and CoLA score compared to only using CBS
In human evaluation on AMT-3, JamDec performs better than all task-specific methods in Obfuscation by at least
Log-depth tree sampling reduces sequential depth from O(n) to O(log n) while preserving the same sampling distribution
the trained alignment model achieves substantial improvements in performance on image-caption retrieval w.r.t. similar methods
retrieval-augmented multi-modal transformers using the trained alignment model improve results on VQA over strong baselines
On the SET task with pre-processed objects, RESOLVE outperforms all baselines up to x better than the second best model and x better than the Abstractor

On MNIST-Math (extracting non-linear weighted subtraction rule from digit image pairs), RESOLVE outperforms other baselines with better accuracy than the transformer and better accuracy than the Abstractor
On 6-element sequence sorting (purely relational sequence-to-sequence task), RESOLVE achieves better accuracy than baselines (x to x better than Relational-Abstractor) on element-wise accuracy metric
Graph attention mechanism achieved a precision of 0.94, a recall of 0.92, and an accuracy of 0.93
The Self-Contradict strategy, which generates different samples to identify self-contradictory content, showed that even GPT-4 was able to induce self-contradictions at rates of 15.7%.
On CFG token-level error robustness test, JEPA-Reasoner maintains higher accuracy than Transformer baseline under 0–30% token corruption across 4 generation steps

Training cost comparison: BPTT incurs linearly increasing wall-clock time with recurrent steps, whereas Thinking States maintains close to constant training time due to teacher forcing, preventing cost penalties at 10+ steps
CoLaGuard 8B matches GuardReasoner 8B in macro-F1 (prompt: 84.23 vs 84.40; response: 83.33 vs 83.13)
CoLaGuard 3B slightly improves prompt macro-F1 over GuardReasoner 3B (84.28 vs 83.91) and response macro-F1 (82.56 vs 82.49)
On graph-hopping, gradient-subspace ablation leaves accuracy intact for most models, but Pause-as-Thought and Coconut show notable flip-rates at high-α values that significantly exceed random control
On TokFSM finite state machine dataset, attention-only codebook model achieves comparable language modeling loss and next token accuracy to original model
On WikiText-103, 24-layer 410M parameter codebook language model with attention codebooks achieves loss and accuracy close to finetuned baseline

Activating topic codes in TinyStories model causes generated text to mention target topics far more frequently: topic code steering achieves 65-100% mention rate across different topics (fire, dragon, slide, flower, baby, princess), compared to baseline mention rates of 0-42.5%
Top-AFA SAE achieves better reconstruction performance than top-k and batch top-k SAEs, surpassing the scaling law boundary reported by Gao et al. (2024).
SAE-derived features achieve macro F1 > 0.8, outperforming hidden-state and BoW baselines
Middle-layer SAE features typically produce higher F1 scores than early- or late-layer features
Binarized and no max pooling of SAE features outperform both hidden-state probes and bag-of-words
XNNTab outperforms interpretable models on nearly all datasets, with the exception of CR, for which XNNTab is on par with Logistic Regression.
Incrementally introducing new knowledge increases hallucinations across Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B and Mistral 7B v03, with the effect being more pronounced with prolonged training.
Single-latent interventions sourced with MoRFI substantially outperform steering with the composite direction (difference-in-means SAE activations between known vs. unknown checkpoints).

For the Unknown direction, the best negatively steered latent gives +33.4 accuracy gain while the best positively steered one gives +21.7 for Llama 3.1 8B; for Mistral 7B the difference is +39.77 vs +24.91 and for Gemma 2 9B +18.46 vs +9.03.
On CLIP embeddings, SoftSAE matches the reconstruction–sparsity performance of TopK and BatchTopK, slightly outperforming BatchTopK at comparable error while remaining close to TopK
On Gemma-2-2B activations, SoftSAE performs comparably to Matryoshka, with a slight advantage in reconstruction at similar sparsity levels
Using SAE Bench absorption and splitting metrics, SoftSAE shows a clear advantage in reducing feature absorption at higher sparsity levels and performs comparably to state-of-the-art Matryoshka SAEs

On Spurious Correlation Removal (SCR) and Targeted Probe Perturbation (TPP) metrics, SoftSAE outperforms TopK and BatchTopK on SCR, and matches or surpasses Matryoshka on TPP at higher sparsity levels
peak activation as the strongest marginal predictor
WikiDYK contains 12,290 facts and 77,180 questions across five question types
PT-RAG achieves the best overall performance across datasets when combining parametric injection with token-based context
MeKi maintains the same generation speed (token/s) as the dense baseline on Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite with KV cache length 10K
GASE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on node classification
GASE achieves a 5× speedup over fine-tuning-based methods
TEMPO reduces leakage from 2~13% to 0.6~3.7% across all conditions
task performance improving 6~13% where strong pre-cutoff signals exist
